claim: Cold-workability was evaluated using the parameter W = (t0 - tmin)/t0 × 100, with t0 initial thickness and tmin minimum thickness before cracking.
evidence_text: the coldworkability parameter W which was defined as W = (t0 - tmin) / t0 × 100, where t0 is the initial thickness and tmin is the minimum thickness before a crack appeared during cold-rolling at room temperature.

claim: Tensile tests were performed at room temperature at a strain rate of 0.5 mm/min on specimens with gauge dimensions 25 mm × 5 mm × 1 mm.
evidence_text: tensile testing at room temperature at a strain rate of 0.5 mm/min using physically polished dumbbell-shaped specimens with gauge dimensions of 25 mm × 5 mm × 1 mm.

claim: The density of fcc Fe–Mn–Al–C(–Cr) alloys was estimated by D_A^fcc = Σ D_i^fcc · x_i, with density parameters D_Fe=8.19, D_Mn=7.19, D_Al=2.70, D_Cr=6.36, D_C=2.85 g/cm³.
evidence_text: By defining the density of the fcc structure of each pure element as a density parameter, the density of Fe–Mn–Al–C(–Cr) γ(fcc) alloy with fcc phase D_A^fcc was estimated using the following equation: D_A^fcc (g/cm³) = Σ D_i^fcc · x_i. … The density parameters used in this study were D_Fe^fcc = 8.19, D_Mn^fcc = 7.19, D_Al^fcc = 2.70, D_Cr^fcc = 6.36 and D_C^fcc = 2.85.

claim: For Fe–20Mn–10Al–5Cr–1.5C alloy, calculated density was 6.64 g/cm³ and measured by Archimedes method was 6.67 g/cm³.
evidence_text: in Fe–20Mn–10Al–5Cr–1.5C (mass%) alloy, the calculated and observed values were 6.64 and 6.67 g/cm³, respectively.

claim: Figure 1 shows iso-density lines on the Fe–20Mn–Al–C phase diagram at 1100°C, indicating γ single-phase alloys with density <7.0 g/cm³ can be obtained by controlling Al and C contents.
evidence_text: Figure 1 shows iso-D_A^fcc contour lines calculated from Eq. (1) on the experimentally determined phase diagram of the Fe–20Mn–Al–C quaternary system at 1100°C … According to Fig. 1, γ single phase alloys with a low density below 7.0 g/cm³ can be obtained by controlling Al and C contents.

claim: For Fe–40Mn–14Al–1C with γ+α two-phase structure, calculated density was 6.31 g/cm³, close to observed 6.13 g/cm³, indicating the estimation method applies to multi-phase alloys.
evidence_text: The density of Fe–40Mn–14Al–1C alloy with the γ+α two-phase structure calculated from Eq.(1) was 6.31 g/cm³, which is slightly different from the observed value of 6.13 g/cm³.

claim: γ(fcc) alloys with density below 7.0 g/cm³ exhibited excellent ductility, and their hardness and tensile strength increased with Al and C content.
evidence_text: The γ(fcc) alloys in both quaternary and quinary systems with a low density of less than 7.0 g/cm³ showed an excellent ductility and their hardness and tensile strength increased with increasing Al and C contents.

claim: High hardness and tensile strength in high-Al and high-C alloys are due to nano-size κ-carbide precipitation with perovskite structure during cooling from annealing temperature, as confirmed by TEM.
evidence_text: TEM observation confirmed that high hardness and tensile strength of the alloys with high Al and C contents are caused by the precipitation of nano-size κ-carbide with perovskite structure during cooling from the annealing temperature.

claim: Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr alloy achieved density 6.51 g/cm³, specific strength >180 MPa·cm³/g, and tensile elongation 40%.
evidence_text: Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr alloy with a density of 6.51 g/cm³ showed a high specific strength of more than 180 MPa·cm³/g with a good tensile elongation of 40%.

claim: The Fe–20Mn–Al–C(–5Cr) alloys exhibit higher specific strength than conventional steels.
evidence_text: The present Fe–20Mn–Al–C(–5Cr) alloys showed a higher specific strength than conventional steels.

claim: The κ phase is ternary carbide Fe3AlCx with perovskite structure, where x ≈ 0.66.
evidence_text: where the κ phase is the ternary carbide Fe3AlCx with perovskite structure and x is around 0.66.

claim: Figure 1 shows iso-density contour lines calculated from Eq. (1) on the experimentally determined Fe–20Mn–Al–C quaternary phase diagram at 1100°C, with the κ phase identified as the ternary carbide Fe₃AlC having perovskite structure and x ≈ 0.66.
evidence_text: Figure 1 shows iso-${ \cdot } D _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathrm { f c c } }$ contour lines calculated from Eq. (1) on the experimentally determined phase diagram of the Fe–20Mn–Al–C quaternary system at $\bar { 1 } 1 0 0 ^ { \circ } C , ^ { 3 \bar { 4 } ) }$ where the k phase is the ternary carbide $\mathrm { F e } _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C } _ { \mathrm { 3 } }$ with perovskite structure and x is round $\dot { 0 } . 6 6 . ^ { 3 5 ) }$

claim: The density of the Fe–40Mn–14Al–1C alloy with γ+α two-phase structure was calculated as 6.31 g/cm³ from Eq. (1), which is slightly different from the observed value of 6.13 g/cm³, indicating the estimation formula can also be applied to non-γ single-phase alloys.
evidence_text: The density of Fe–40Mn–14Al–1C alloy with the $\gamma + \alpha$ two-phase structure calculated from Eq.(1) was 6.31 g/cm3 , which is slightly different from the observed value of $6 . 1 3 \mathrm { g } / \mathrm { c m } ^ { 3 }$.

claim: The Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C γ single-phase alloy exhibited a hardness of 466 Hv, a high yield strength of 850 MPa, and an upper yield point followed by almost constant flow stress.
evidence_text: the Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C g alloy with a hardness of 466 Hv showed a high yield strength of 850 MPa and an upper yield point followed by almost constant flow stress.

claim: The Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C alloy showed a high hardness of 413 Hv but was very brittle, with cold-workability of 0%, due to the formation of a certain amount of κ phase which stably exists at the annealing temperature.
evidence_text: The Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C alloy showed a high hardness of 413 Hv but was very brittle. This is due to the formation of a certain amount of k phase, which stably exists at the annealing temperature.

claim: The Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr γ alloy exhibited a high hardness of 431 Hv, a good cold-workability of 71%, and an upper yield point followed by almost constant flow stress, attributed to suppression of κ-carbide by Cr.
evidence_text: The Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr g alloy ... exhibited a high hardness of 431 Hv with a good cold-workability of 71%. This means that k-carbide is suppressed by the addition of Cr. In addition, the Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr g alloy as well as the Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C quaternary g alloy showed an upper yield point followed by almost constant flow stress.

claim: Quinary γ+α alloys with an Al content of 13 mass% and a low D_A^fcc below 6.5 g/cm³ exhibited high strength and relatively large elongation.
evidence_text: The quinary y+α alloys with Al content of 13 mass%, which possesses a low $D _ { \mathrm { A } } ^ { \mathrm { f c c } }$ below $6 . 5 \mathrm { g } / \mathrm { c m } ^ { 3 }$, exhibited high strength and relatively large elongation.

claim: These alloys showed a high specific strength of over 120 MPa·cm³/g.
evidence_text: Fe–20Mn–Al–C(–5Cr) alloys with Al content below 13 mass% and C content over 1.0 mass% showed a high specific strength of over 120 MPa·cm³/g

claim: This alloy exhibited a high specific strength of over 180 MPa·cm³/g with good tensile elongation of around 30% and a density of 6.51 g/cm³.
evidence_text: the Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr alloy with D_A^fcc = 6.51 g/cm³ exhibited a high specific strength of over 180 MPa·cm³/g with good tensile elongation of around 30%.

claim: In Fe–20Mn–10.5Al–1.2C alloy, the precipitation of a very fine κ phase occurs during quenching from temperatures above 900°C.
evidence_text: Ishida et al. have reported that in Fe–20Mn–10.5Al–1.2C alloy, the precipitation of a very fine k phase occurs during quenching from temperatures above 900°C.

claim: Water quenching provides a cooling rate of about 1100°C/s, while air cooling provides about 15°C/s after annealing at 1100°C.
evidence_text: the cooling rates of the water quenching and the air cooling were about 1100°C/s and about 15°C/s, respectively.

claim: The water-quenched specimen showed almost linear work-hardening; the air-cooled specimen showed almost zero work-hardening rate at plastic strain below 5% and then work-hardening.
evidence_text: The water-quenched specimen showed an almost linear work-hardening, while the air-cooled specimen showed an almost zero work-hardening rate at a small plastic strain region below 5 % and then work-hardening with increasing plastic strain.

claim: Dark field imaging of (100)κ shows very fine κ precipitates slightly confirmed in WQ, whereas cuboidal κ precipitates aligned along <001> directions are clearly observed in AC; the AC microstructure resembles γ+γ' in Ni-based superalloys.
evidence_text: In the water-quenched specimen, very fine k phase precipitates are slightly confirmed, while the cuboidal k phase precipitates aligned in the <001> directions are clearly observed in the air-cooled specimen. The microstructure observed in the air-cooled Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C–5Cr alloy is very similar to the morphology observed in Ni-based superalloys with the γ+γ' two-phase.

claim: A high specific strength of about 200 MPa·cm³/g was obtained by air cooling from 1100°C.
evidence_text: In the Fe–20Mn–13Al–1.3C–5Cr γ+α alloy, a high specific strength of about 200 MPa·cm³/g was obtained by air cooling.

claim: This alloy showed specific strength over 230 MPa·cm³/g with elongation over 10%.
evidence_text: The 30% cold-rolled Fe–20Mn–11Al–1.8C–5Cr g alloy showed specific strength over 230 MPa·cm³/g with elongation over 10%.

claim: These alloys possessed hardness over 650 Hv and specific strength over 300 MPa·cm³/g, but elongation drastically decreased.
evidence_text: the Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C and Fe–20Mn–10Al–1.5C–5Cr alloys cold rolled at reduction ratios more than 90% possessed a high hardness of over 650 Hv and a high specific strength of over 300 MPa·cm³/g, although elongation drastically decreased.

claim: The present Fe–Mn–Al–C(–Cr) alloys showed a higher specific strength than conventional steels such as austenitic, ferritic, martensitic, precipitation hardened, dual-phase and super toughness steels.
evidence_text: It is seen that the present Fe–Mn–Al–C(–Cr) alloys showed a higher specific strength than the conventional steels such as austenitic,41) ferritic,41) martensitic,41) precipitation hardened,41) dual-phase42) and super toughness steels.42)

claim: TRIP steels possess a much higher specific strength than the present alloys but require a complicated thermomechanical process, whereas the present alloys achieve both high specific strength and large tensile elongation through simple processes controlling annealing temperature and cooling rate.
evidence_text: TRIP steels reported by Zakay et al. possess a much higher specific strength than the present alloys. However, the high strength of the TRIP steels can be obtained through a complicated thermomechanical process. On the other hand, the present alloys have an advantage in that both high specific strength and large tensile elongation can be obtained through simple processes controlling the annealing temperature and cooling rate.

claim: In γ alloys, hardness, yield and tensile strengths increased with increasing Al and C contents, while tensile elongation decreased. The γ alloys with high Al and C contents showed a high yield strength and an upper yield point followed by almost constant flow stress. γ+α two-phase alloys also exhibited high strength and good ductility by controlling the α volume fraction.
evidence_text: (1) The hardness, yield and tensile strengths of the g alloys increased with increasing Al and C contents, while the tensile elongation decreased. The g alloys with high Al and C contents showed a high yield strength and an upper yield point followed by almost constant flow stress. It was found that γ+α two-phase alloys also exhibited high strength, together with good ductility, by controlling the a volume fraction.

claim: The γ/γ+κ phase boundary shifted toward the high-C side, suppressing the precipitation of κ carbide, by the addition of Cr. The γ+α two-phase region widened to the high-Al side by the addition of Cr.
evidence_text: (2) Microstructural observation showed that the γ/γ+κ phase boundary shifted toward the high-C side suppressing the precipitation of k carbide by the addition of Cr. It was also found that g/ghigh-C side and that the γ+α two-phase region widened to the high-Al side by the addition of Cr.
